author: @zeroumashi2947
text:
replace the power drill with a 24v motor from ebay and some batteries and a bridge rectifier and replace the brake cable with an electronic throttle or a potentiometer with a return spring attached to the knob and you have yourself an e-bike.
you'll also need something to act as a clutch to disengage the motor when the motor is not active otherwise you'll get unintended wear on the motor and it'll backfeed a voltage to the battery.

author: @DeliciousDeBlair

If I got a cheap beat up one where the batteries died, from a thrift store, then sure!

author: @scotttovey

The only difference is that you would want a single controller for both motors and you would want a couple of drills that have variable speed control.

author: @CraigDavies.
text: All these rub on the tyre motors are very inefficient. Don't listen to me just go and read the threads on pedlecs. They will advise on the best bikes and kits. Don't forget the only control legal in the UK is operated by the pedals.

replies.text: in the uk it is speed limited mate - throttle controls are 3.7mph - but this isn't about the best - it is really a toy to have fun with

replies.author: @another3997
replies.text: The amount of assistance on many e-bikes is usually controlled via a selector on the handlebar. From zero assistance to full boost and any number of steps between.

author: @jasonhaymanonthedrawingboard
text: This is great! You can extend the range by 20- 30% by rigging the contacts with a larger battery from a motorcycle. Most drills are 18v or 24v. I’ve a skate wheel and a strip down drill. I wonder I really do, how simple things can be made? I bet you could improve the torc ratios a we bit by changing the diameter of the drive wheel. Well done lads keep it up.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice

text: This could be used  as a generator to recharge the battery. Especially when going downhill reclaiming energy from gravity.All it needs is a switching system and a diode to go from drive mode to charge mode.

author: @RaperJason
text: Good fun and a proof of concept please don't get me wrong I enjoy the idea But if you ride that on the road it is not classed as an E bike it is classed as an electric motorcycle you will attract the heavy weight of the law in the form of fines and possibly the loss of your driving licence A throttle is not alowed on Ebike it has to be controled by the pedals a lot of the over engineering is to comply with the letter of the law Maybe draconian and wrong but no insurance tax licence and a not type approved vehicle = 12 points + £300 fine

author: @Jannie303
text:
Awesome idea, just like my old VeloSolex but with electricity. However I've seen few contraptions that use electric drills as the motive power and I've always had concerns about:
1) overheating the drill with continuous use
2) the longevity of the bushings in the drill. A lot of them seem to have bushings rather than bearings for the chuck and I've managed to wear out a few just with relatively normal use.

Do you have any thoughts?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it really depends on the drill you use - but this is a toy really

replies.author: @Jannie303
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering fair enough

author: @ejkozan

author: @AndreaDingbatt
text:
Oh boy, this is now going to be such an important issue for me now!!
My boy Jasper, has a crutiate ligament problem, and Osteoarthritis in both knees...
I have an E-bike, but it's so heavy, and I'm disabled...
I don't know whether to have a sidecar made, or have an E-Trike made...
Any thoughts?!
Thank you so much for the great information and positivity!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i making an etrike from paper in the next project mate - it might be one for you to think about

replies.author: @AndreaDingbatt
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Oh my gosh!!
That sounds like just the ticket!!
My boy Jasper is my Hero and has been looking after me for nearly 10years!!
And, I hope to be able to return the favour!!
My brains turned to mush, last week, when he seemed to be paralysed in the back legs!
I can't even think straight, this little Mutt, indicates when I am about to have a seizure, picks up cash, when I drop it, has hung onto my wallet, when someone pretending to help me, tried to help themselves!!
So, it's my turn to help him!!
I love the E-bike, because, I become just another bleeping cyclist, & that's very liberating, for a wheelchair user!!
Thank you so much,, people like you, make this world a better place!!
Andrea, Jasper and George the Pigeon. XxX.

author: @shay4578
text:
It wouldn't hurt to have the thing detach from the wheel when not in use.
Having it stay connected at all times means it will hinder movement more then assist most of the time.

Nice work showing how the general idea is applied, though.

Keep up the good work.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice point mate - cheers

replies.author: @jllaine
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering A $10 one way bearing pressed into the castor wheel instead of the plate, and then weld the axle to the inner ring and you have coasting ability with the drill off.

replies.author: @shay4578
replies.text:
@jllaine  Nice workaround, mate.
Way to make it simple.

author: @1upPaintingAB
text:
If these ebikes keep catching on we will eventually have to insure and register them. Ingenious idea though.
I hope the bystanders enjoyed the laughter as much as me.

replies.author: @William_Hada
replies.text: I hope you are joking about that! Or are you an insurance salesman? LOL.

replies.author: @shay4578
replies.text:
In Japan, regular bikes are registered.
It works wonderfully.
If you park your bike somewhere you shouldn't, a fine is sent to your home address.
I think bikes with no number get scrapped without notice.
Forcing parents to put insurance on the kids' bikes will be redundant if they are already paying for health insurance like in Israel.

replies.author: @timothygorman7528
replies.text: I live in the States, Pennsylvania.   It used to be a 48cc 2-stroke motor on a bike was legal; now ANY combustion motor on a bike is considered a Moped - no problem, you now have to have at least a Class C drivers license, take the bike to a special inspection station to clear it for a title, apply for and receive a title, and then register the bike.   Yep.   E-bikes are limited to 750 Watt motors (total BS)...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: glad you liked it mate

replies.author: @another3997
replies.text: @timothygorman7528 You say it's total BS. Some would say it's a good idea putting limits on E-bikes. The vast majority of bicycles aren't built or maintained to the same standards as other motorised vehicles. Do you trust kids, teenagers and young adults to use a fast e-bike safely and sensibly, without any training, on pavements and roads full of pedestrians, cars, trucks and bicycles etc?
